expanded_description: Understanding the fundamental properties, definitions, and behaviors of acids and bases—the two most important classes of corrosive substances in chemistry.

core_concept.principle: Acids and bases are defined by their ability to donate or accept protons (H⁺ ions) and exhibit characteristic properties including taste, reactivity with metals, conductivity, and effects on indicators.
core_concept.explanation: Acids and bases represent opposite ends of a chemical spectrum. Acids taste sour, react with metals to produce hydrogen gas, turn blue litmus red, and conduct electricity in solution. Bases taste bitter, feel slippery, turn red litmus blue, and also conduct electricity. These properties arise from their ability to produce H⁺ or OH⁻ ions in aqueous solution, enabling them to participate in neutralization reactions and pH-dependent processes.

image.alt: Diagram showing characteristic properties of acids and bases with indicator color changes
image.caption: Acids and bases exhibit opposite properties and react to form salt and water

sections.introduction: Acids and bases are among the most important and commonly encountered substances in chemistry. From the citric acid in lemons to the sodium hydroxide in drain cleaners, these substances play crucial roles in biological systems, industrial processes, and everyday life. Understanding their properties and behaviors is fundamental to mastering chemistry.

key_concept.definition: Acids are substances that produce hydrogen ions (H⁺) in aqueous solution; bases are substances that produce hydroxide ions (OH⁻) in aqueous solution or accept H⁺ ions.
key_concept.context: The Arrhenius definition provides the foundation, but broader definitions (Brønsted-Lowry, Lewis) extend these concepts to non-aqueous systems and proton transfer reactions.

principles.statement: Acids increase H⁺ concentration in water; bases increase OH⁻ concentration or decrease H⁺ concentration.
principles.application: This ion production explains conductivity, reactivity, and pH effects of acids and bases.
principles.example: HCl(aq) → H⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq) (acid); NaOH(aq) → Na⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) (base)
principles.analogy: Like adding players to a team—acids add H⁺ 'players' to solution; bases add OH⁻ 'players' or remove H⁺ 'players'.

principles.statement: Acids and bases exhibit opposite characteristic properties that allow them to neutralize each other.
principles.application: These opposing properties enable identification, classification, and quantitative analysis of acids and bases.
principles.example: Acids turn litmus red; bases turn litmus blue. Acids react with metals; bases do not. Acids taste sour; bases taste bitter.
principles.analogy: Like hot and cold—opposite sensations that can balance each other when mixed.

components.definition: Acids and bases are characterized by observable properties, ion production, and reactivity patterns.
components.interpretation: The presence of H⁺ or OH⁻ ions determines acidic or basic behavior; concentration determines strength of effect.

image.alt: Table comparing acid and base properties with indicator colors
image.caption: Acids and bases exhibit characteristic opposing properties

worked_examples.title: Identifying Acids and Bases from Properties
worked_examples.problem: A substance turns blue litmus paper red, reacts with magnesium to produce bubbles, and has a sour taste. Is it an acid or base? Explain.

step_by_step.explanation: Blue litmus turning red is characteristic of acids
Reaction with active metals (Mg, Zn, Fe) producing H₂ gas is characteristic of acids
Sour taste is characteristic of acids (though tasting chemicals is unsafe!)
All three properties indicate acidic character

worked_examples.final_answer: The substance is an acid (all observed properties match acid characteristics)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Characteristic properties allow identification of acids and bases.

worked_examples.title: Writing Ionization Equations
worked_examples.problem: Write the ionization equation for nitric acid (HNO₃) in water and identify the ions produced.

step_by_step.explanation: Nitric acid is HNO₃
Acids produce H⁺ and anion in water
H⁺ is the hydrogen ion; NO₃⁻ is the nitrate ion

worked_examples.final_answer: HNO₃(aq) → H⁺(aq) + NO₃⁻(aq); produces hydrogen and nitrate ions
worked_examples.concept_applied: Acid ionization produces H⁺ and the corresponding anion.

sections.key_concept: Acids exhibit a consistent set of observable properties that distinguish them from other substances and from bases.

classifications.value: Observable characteristics detectable by human senses (though tasting chemicals is unsafe!)
characteristics: Sour taste (citric acid in lemons, acetic acid in vinegar)
Pungent odor for some acids (HCl, HNO₃)
Causes stinging sensation on skin (corrosive)
classifications.behavior: These properties arise from H⁺ ions interacting with biological receptors

examples.explanation: Contain citric acid; sour taste signals acidity
Contains acetic acid; sour taste and pungent smell

classifications.value: Characteristic chemical reactions that identify acidic behavior
characteristics: React with active metals to produce H₂ gas
React with carbonates to produce CO₂ gas
Neutralize bases to form salt and water
classifications.behavior: H⁺ ions participate in redox reactions with metals and acid-base reactions with bases/carbonates

examples.explanation: $\text{Zn}(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)$; bubbles of H₂ gas
$\text{CaCO}_3(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{CaCl}_2(aq) + \text{CO}_2(g) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$; CO₂ bubbles

classifications.value: Color changes with acid-base indicators that signal acidic conditions
characteristics: Turn blue litmus red
Turn phenolphthalein colorless
Turn universal indicator red/orange/yellow
classifications.behavior: Indicators are weak acids/bases that change structure (and color) with H⁺ concentration

examples.explanation: Blue litmus → red in acid; red litmus stays red in acid
pH 1-3: red; pH 4-6: orange/yellow in acidic range

classifications.value: Ability to conduct electricity in aqueous solution
characteristics: Conduct electricity due to mobile ions
Strong acids conduct better than weak acids
Conductivity proportional to ion concentration
classifications.behavior: H⁺ and anion ions carry electric current through solution

examples.explanation: Fully ionized; high conductivity
Partially ionized; lower conductivity

worked_examples.title: Predicting Acid-Metal Reaction
worked_examples.problem: Will hydrochloric acid react with copper metal? Explain using acid reactivity principles.

step_by_step.explanation: Acids react with metals above hydrogen in the activity series
step_by_step.calculation: Activity series: K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Sn > Pb > H > Cu > Ag > Au

step_by_step.explanation: Copper is below hydrogen in the activity series
Metals below H do not react with acids to produce H₂
step_by_step.calculation: Prediction: No reaction between HCl and Cu

step_by_step.explanation: Only metals more reactive than hydrogen displace H⁺ from acids
step_by_step.calculation: Cu cannot reduce H⁺ to H₂

worked_examples.final_answer: No reaction; copper is less reactive than hydrogen and cannot displace H⁺ from HCl
worked_examples.concept_applied: Acid-metal reactions depend on metal position in activity series.

worked_examples.title: Using Indicators to Identify Acids
worked_examples.problem: Three unlabeled solutions are tested with litmus paper: Solution A turns blue litmus red; Solution B turns red litmus blue; Solution C causes no color change. Classify each solution.

step_by_step.explanation: Blue litmus → red in acid; red litmus → blue in base; no change in neutral
Turns blue litmus red → acidic
Turns red litmus blue → basic
No color change with either litmus → neutral
step_by_step.calculation: Solution C: Neutral (e.g., pure water, salt solution)

worked_examples.final_answer: A: Acid; B: Base; C: Neutral
worked_examples.concept_applied: Indicator color changes allow classification of solutions as acidic, basic, or neutral.

sections.introduction: Bases exhibit properties that are generally opposite to those of acids. Understanding these characteristics enables identification, safe handling, and prediction of base behavior in chemical reactions.

key_concept.definition: Bases are substances that produce hydroxide ions (OH⁻) in aqueous solution or accept H⁺ ions, exhibiting characteristic properties including bitter taste, slippery feel, and indicator responses.
key_concept.context: Like acids, bases conduct electricity in solution due to mobile ions; however, their reactivity patterns and sensory properties differ markedly from acids.

categories.description: Observable characteristics of bases (though direct sensory testing of chemicals is unsafe)
categories.detailed_mechanism: OH⁻ ions interact with biological receptors to produce characteristic sensations

examples.explanation: Bases taste bitter (e.g., baking soda, soap); never taste laboratory chemicals
Bases feel slippery or soapy on skin due to saponification of skin oils
Strong bases corrode skin and tissues; handle with protective equipment

categories.detailed_mechanism: OH⁻ ions participate in neutralization reactions and can react with certain metal ions

examples.explanation: $\text{NaOH}(aq) + \text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$; neutralization
$\text{Cu}^{2+}(aq) + 2\text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{Cu(OH)}_2(s)$; precipitate formation
$\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}(aq) + \text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NH}_3(g) + \text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$; ammonia gas released

categories.description: Color changes with acid-base indicators in basic conditions
categories.detailed_mechanism: Indicators change structure in response to OH⁻ concentration or low H⁺ concentration

examples.explanation: Red litmus → blue in base; blue litmus stays blue in base
Colorless in acid/neutral; pink/fuchsia in base (pH > 8.2)
pH 8-10: green/blue; pH 11-14: purple in basic range

categories.description: Ability to conduct electricity in aqueous solution
categories.detailed_mechanism: OH⁻ and cation ions carry electric current; conductivity depends on degree of ionization

examples.explanation: Fully dissociated; high conductivity
Partially ionized; lower conductivity

worked_examples.title: Predicting Base Reactions
worked_examples.problem: Write the balanced equation for the reaction between sodium hydroxide and ammonium chloride. What gas is produced?

step_by_step.explanation: NaOH is a strong base; NH₄Cl is an ammonium salt
Bases react with ammonium salts to release ammonia gas
step_by_step.calculation: General: Base + NH₄⁺ → NH₃(g) + H₂O + salt

step_by_step.explanation: Include states and balance atoms
Ammonia (NH₃) is produced; has characteristic pungent odor

worked_examples.final_answer: NaOH(aq) + NH₄Cl(aq) → NH₃(g) + NaCl(aq) + H₂O(l); ammonia gas is produced
worked_examples.concept_applied: Bases react with ammonium salts to release ammonia gas.

worked_examples.title: Using Phenolphthalein to Identify Bases
worked_examples.problem: A solution turns phenolphthalein indicator pink. What can you conclude about the solution's pH and classification?

step_by_step.explanation: Phenolphthalein is colorless in acid/neutral; pink in base
Pink color indicates basic conditions
Phenolphthalein doesn't distinguish between weak and strong bases; only indicates pH > 8.2
step_by_step.calculation: Further testing needed to determine base strength or exact pH

worked_examples.final_answer: Solution is basic (pH > 8.2); phenolphthalein pink indicates OH⁻ presence
worked_examples.concept_applied: Indicator color changes provide qualitative information about solution acidity/basicity.

sections.section_title: Common Acids and Bases in Everyday Life
sections.key_concept: Acids and bases are ubiquitous in daily life, from food and beverages to cleaning products and biological systems. Recognizing common examples connects chemical principles to real-world experience.

classifications.value: Frequently encountered acidic substances in household, biological, and industrial contexts
characteristics: Often have 'acid' in name or formula starts with H
Many are weak acids safe for consumption in dilute form
Strong acids require careful handling
classifications.behavior: Acid strength and concentration determine applications and safety considerations

examples.explanation: Weak acid; main component of vinegar (5% solution); used in cooking and cleaning
Weak acid; found in citrus fruits; used as food preservative and flavoring
Strong acid; present in stomach gastric juice (0.5%); concentrated form used in industry
Strong acid; used in car batteries, fertilizer production; highly corrosive
Weak acid; formed when CO₂ dissolves in water; gives carbonated beverages their tang

classifications.value: Frequently encountered basic substances in household, biological, and industrial contexts
characteristics: Often contain OH in formula or end in '-hydroxide'
Many weak bases are safe for household use
Strong bases are caustic and require protective handling
classifications.behavior: Base strength and concentration determine applications and safety precautions

examples.explanation: Strong base; 'lye' or 'caustic soda'; used in drain cleaners, soap making; highly corrosive
Weak base; household ammonia cleaner (5-10% solution); pungent odor; effective for grease
Weak base; 'baking soda'; used in baking, antacids, deodorizing; mild and safe
Weak base; 'milk of magnesia'; used as antacid and laxative; low solubility limits causticity
Moderate base; 'slaked lime'; used in mortar, water treatment, soil pH adjustment

classifications.value: Acids and bases essential for life processes
characteristics: Tightly regulated pH in biological systems
Buffer systems maintain stable pH
Enzyme activity depends on pH
classifications.behavior: Biological acids/bases function in narrow pH ranges; deviations disrupt physiological processes

examples.explanation: pH ~1.5-3.5; activates digestive enzymes; kills pathogens; protected by mucus lining
Bicarbonate buffer system maintains blood pH at 7.35-7.45; critical for oxygen transport
Cytoplasm pH ~7.2; organelles have specialized pH (lysosomes pH ~4.5-5.0 for enzyme activity)

worked_examples.title: Identifying Household Acids and Bases
worked_examples.problem: Classify each household substance as acid, base, or neutral: (a) lemon juice, (b) baking soda solution, (c) table salt solution, (d) vinegar, (e) ammonia cleaner.

step_by_step.explanation: Contains citric acid; sour taste; turns litmus red
NaHCO₃ is a weak base; bitter taste; turns litmus blue
NaCl is neutral salt; no effect on litmus; pH ~7
Contains acetic acid; sour taste; turns litmus red
NH₃ solution; pungent odor; turns litmus blue; slippery feel

worked_examples.concept_applied: Common household substances can be classified as acids, bases, or neutral based on properties.

worked_examples.title: Safety Considerations for Acids and Bases
worked_examples.problem: Explain why concentrated sulfuric acid and sodium hydroxide require special handling precautions, while vinegar and baking soda are safe for household use.

step_by_step.explanation: H₂SO₄ and NaOH are strong electrolytes; fully ionized in water; high [H⁺] or [OH⁻]
step_by_step.calculation: Strong acids/bases: highly reactive and corrosive

step_by_step.explanation: Concentrated H₂SO₄ (~18 M) and NaOH (~19 M) vs. vinegar (~0.8 M acetic acid) and baking soda (~1 M NaHCO₃)
Strong acids dehydrate tissues; strong bases saponify fats; both cause severe chemical burns
step_by_step.calculation: Weak acids/bases at low concentrations cause minimal tissue damage

step_by_step.explanation: Always wear PPE with strong acids/bases; dilute household products are formulated for safe use
step_by_step.calculation: Concentration and strength determine hazard level

worked_examples.final_answer: Strong acids/bases at high concentrations are highly corrosive; weak acids/bases at low concentrations are safe for household use with normal precautions
worked_examples.concept_applied: Acid/base strength and concentration determine safety requirements and handling procedures.

sections.introduction: The most characteristic reaction of acids and bases is neutralization—their mutual reaction to form salt and water. This reaction is fundamental to chemistry, biology, and industrial applications.

key_concept.definition: Neutralization is the reaction between an acid and a base to produce a salt and water: $\text{acid} + \text{base} \rightarrow \text{salt} + \text{water}$.
key_concept.context: The net ionic equation for strong acid-strong base neutralization is $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, showing that H⁺ and OH⁻ combine to form water.

principles.statement: Neutralization occurs when H⁺ ions from acid combine with OH⁻ ions from base to form water molecules.
principles.application: This principle explains why stoichiometric amounts of acid and base neutralize each other completely.
principles.analogy: Like matching puzzle pieces—H⁺ and OH⁻ fit together perfectly to make neutral water.

principles.statement: The cation from the base and anion from the acid combine to form an ionic salt in neutralization reactions.
principles.application: Salts may be soluble or insoluble; soluble salts remain dissociated in solution.
principles.example: In $\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$, Na⁺ (from base) and Cl⁻ (from acid) form NaCl salt
principles.analogy: Like dance partners switching—H⁺ pairs with OH⁻ to make water; the remaining ions pair to make salt.

worked_examples.title: Writing Neutralization Equations
worked_examples.problem: Write the molecular, complete ionic, and net ionic equations for the reaction between sulfuric acid and potassium hydroxide.

step_by_step.explanation: Balance acid + base → salt + water
Dissociate all strong electrolytes; keep water as molecule
K⁺ and SO₄²⁻ appear unchanged on both sides
Show only reacting species

worked_examples.final_answer: Molecular: H₂SO₄ + 2KOH → K₂SO₄ + 2H₂O; Net ionic: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O
worked_examples.concept_applied: Neutralization net ionic equation shows H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O regardless of specific acid/base.

worked_examples.title: Predicting Neutralization Products
worked_examples.problem: Predict the products and write the balanced equation for the reaction between nitric acid and calcium hydroxide.

step_by_step.explanation: HNO₃ provides H⁺ and NO₃⁻; Ca(OH)₂ provides Ca²⁺ and OH⁻
Cation from base (Ca²⁺) + anion from acid (NO₃⁻) → Ca(NO₃)₂
Acid + base → salt + water; balance H and O
Count atoms on both sides

worked_examples.concept_applied: Neutralization always produces salt (from cation + anion) and water (from H⁺ + OH⁻).

sections.section_title: Safety and Handling of Acids and Bases
sections.key_concept: Proper handling of acids and bases is essential for laboratory safety. Understanding their corrosive nature and appropriate precautions prevents accidents and injuries.

classifications.value: Essential safety gear for working with acids and bases
characteristics: Chemical splash goggles (not regular glasses)
Lab coat or apron
Chemical-resistant gloves (nitrile, neoprene)
Closed-toe shoes
classifications.behavior: PPE creates barrier between corrosive substances and skin/eyes; always wear before handling

examples.explanation: Acids/bases can cause permanent eye damage; goggles prevent splashes from reaching eyes
Nitrile gloves resist most acids/bases; latex degrades with some chemicals

classifications.value: Safe procedure for preparing dilute solutions from concentrated acids/bases
characteristics: Always add acid to water, never water to acid
Add slowly with stirring
Use heat-resistant container for exothermic dilutions
classifications.behavior: Adding water to concentrated acid causes violent boiling and splashing; adding acid to water allows heat to dissipate safely

examples.explanation: Add concentrated H₂SO₄ slowly to water with stirring; solution heats up significantly
Add solid NaOH or concentrated solution to water; dissolution is exothermic

classifications.value: Procedures for safely managing acid or base spills
characteristics: Neutralize small spills with appropriate agent
Absorb with inert material
Dispose as hazardous waste
Evacuate and call for help with large spills
classifications.behavior: Quick, appropriate response minimizes damage and exposure risk

examples.explanation: Neutralize with sodium bicarbonate; absorb with paper towels; dispose properly
Neutralize with dilute acetic acid or citric acid; absorb; dispose properly

classifications.value: Immediate response to acid or base contact with skin or eyes
characteristics: Flush with copious water for 15+ minutes
Remove contaminated clothing
Seek medical attention
Do not neutralize on skin (generates heat)
classifications.behavior: Dilution with water is fastest way to reduce damage; neutralization on skin causes additional thermal injury

examples.explanation: Immediately flush with running water for 15 minutes; remove contaminated clothing; seek medical help
Use eyewash station immediately; hold eyelids open; flush for 15+ minutes; seek emergency care

worked_examples.title: Safe Dilution Procedure
worked_examples.problem: Describe the safe procedure for preparing 500 mL of 1.0 M HCl from concentrated (12 M) HCl.

step_by_step.step: Calculate volume of concentrated acid needed
step_by_step.explanation: Use dilution formula: $C_1V_1 = C_2V_2$
Wear goggles, gloves, lab coat; work in fume hood if possible

step_by_step.step: Add water to volumetric flask first
step_by_step.explanation: Place ~300 mL distilled water in 500 mL volumetric flask
Slowly add 42 mL concentrated HCl to flask with gentle swirling; solution will heat up
step_by_step.calculation: Add acid to water, never water to acid

step_by_step.explanation: Allow solution to cool to room temperature, then add water to 500 mL mark; mix thoroughly
step_by_step.calculation: Cooling before final volume ensures accuracy (volume changes with temperature)

worked_examples.final_answer: Add 42 mL concentrated HCl slowly to ~300 mL water in 500 mL flask, cool, dilute to mark, mix
worked_examples.concept_applied: Always add concentrated acid to water, not water to acid, to prevent violent reactions.

worked_examples.title: First Aid Response to Acid Splash
worked_examples.problem: A student accidentally splashes dilute HCl on their forearm. What immediate actions should be taken?

step_by_step.explanation: Go to safety shower or sink immediately; flush affected area with copious running water
step_by_step.calculation: Flush for minimum 15 minutes to dilute and remove acid

step_by_step.explanation: Carefully remove any clothing that contacted the acid while flushing continues
step_by_step.calculation: Prevents continued exposure from soaked fabric

step_by_step.explanation: Avoid applying base to neutralize acid on skin; neutralization reaction generates heat and can cause thermal burns
step_by_step.calculation: Water dilution is safest and most effective first aid

step_by_step.explanation: Even after flushing, have exposure evaluated by medical professional; report chemical involved
step_by_step.calculation: Some damage may not be immediately apparent; professional assessment ensures proper care

worked_examples.final_answer: Flush with water 15+ minutes, remove contaminated clothing, do not neutralize on skin, seek medical attention
worked_examples.concept_applied: Immediate dilution with water is the most effective first aid for acid/base exposure.

sections.key_concept: Misconceptions about acids and bases can lead to incorrect predictions and unsafe practices. Clarifying these misunderstandings builds accurate chemical understanding.

classifications.value: Assuming all acids are highly corrosive like sulfuric or hydrochloric acid
characteristics: Confuses acid strength with acid presence
Overlooks weak acids in food/biology
Leads to unnecessary fear or inappropriate handling
classifications.behavior: Acid strength (degree of ionization) and concentration determine hazard level; many weak acids are safe in dilute form

examples.explanation: Weak acid; safe for consumption; not corrosive at food concentrations
HCl is strong acid but diluted to ~0.5% in stomach; essential for digestion

classifications.type: Error: Bases Are Always 'Opposite' of Acids in All Ways
classifications.value: Assuming bases have exactly opposite properties in every respect
characteristics: Overgeneralizes indicator behavior
Ignores that both conduct electricity
Misses that both can be corrosive
classifications.behavior: Acids and bases are opposite in H⁺/OH⁻ production and some properties, but share characteristics like conductivity and potential corrosivity

examples.explanation: Both acids and bases conduct electricity in solution due to mobile ions
Strong bases (NaOH) are as corrosive as strong acids (HCl); both require careful handling

classifications.type: Error: Neutralization Always Produces Neutral pH
classifications.value: Assuming acid-base reactions always result in pH = 7
characteristics: Ignores salt hydrolysis
Overlooks weak acid/weak base combinations
Simplifies complex equilibria
classifications.behavior: Strong acid + strong base → neutral salt (pH 7); weak acid + strong base → basic salt (pH > 7); strong acid + weak base → acidic salt (pH < 7)

examples.explanation: Produces sodium acetate; acetate ion hydrolyzes to give basic solution (pH > 7)
Produces ammonium chloride; ammonium ion hydrolyzes to give acidic solution (pH < 7)

characteristics: Concentration = amount per volume; strength = degree of ionization
A dilute strong acid can be less hazardous than concentrated weak acid
Critical distinction for safety and reactivity predictions
classifications.behavior: Strong/weak refers to ionization extent; concentrated/dilute refers to amount per volume

examples.process: 0.001 M HCl vs. 10 M acetic acid
examples.explanation: HCl is strong but very dilute; acetic acid is weak but concentrated; hazard depends on both factors

worked_examples.title: Error Analysis: Concentration vs. Strength
worked_examples.problem: A student claims that 0.001 M HCl is more hazardous than 10 M acetic acid because HCl is a 'stronger' acid. Evaluate this claim.

step_by_step.explanation: Strength = degree of ionization; HCl is strong (~100% ionized); acetic acid is weak (~1% ionized)
Concentration = moles per liter; 0.001 M HCl has very low [H⁺]; 10 M acetic acid has high total acid
HCl: [H⁺] ≈ 0.001 M (fully ionized); Acetic acid: [H⁺] ≈ √(Ka × C) ≈ √(1.8×10⁻⁵ × 10) ≈ 0.013 M
Hazard depends on [H⁺], total acid, and other factors; concentrated weak acid can be more hazardous than dilute strong acid
step_by_step.calculation: Claim is incorrect; 10 M acetic acid likely more hazardous than 0.001 M HCl

worked_examples.final_answer: Claim is incorrect; acid hazard depends on both strength AND concentration; concentrated weak acid can be more hazardous than dilute strong acid
worked_examples.concept_applied: Acid strength (ionization) and concentration (amount/volume) are distinct concepts that both affect reactivity and hazard.

worked_examples.title: Acid-Base Concept Checklist
worked_examples.problem: Outline a systematic approach for analyzing acid-base questions.

step_by_step.explanation: Is it an acid, base, or salt? Check formula (H-starting = likely acid; OH-containing = likely base)
Strong acids: HCl, HBr, HI, HNO₃, H₂SO₄, HClO₄; Strong bases: Group 1 hydroxides, heavy Group 2 hydroxides
step_by_step.calculation: Others are weak; weak acids/bases partially ionize

step_by_step.explanation: Concentrated vs. dilute affects reactivity and hazard; calculate [H⁺] or [OH⁻] if needed
Use ion production to predict indicator response, metal reactivity, neutralization behavior
Strong/concentrated acids/bases require PPE and careful handling; weak/dilute may be safer
Check predictions against experimental data, pH measurements, or known behavior

worked_examples.final_answer: Follow 6-step checklist: identify type, determine strength, consider concentration, predict behavior, apply safety, verify
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic analysis prevents misconceptions and ensures accurate acid-base understanding.

summary: Acids and bases are fundamental classes of chemical substances defined by their ability to produce H⁺ or OH⁻ ions in aqueous solution (Arrhenius definition). Acids exhibit sour taste, react with active metals to produce H₂ gas, turn blue litmus red, and conduct electricity; bases exhibit bitter taste, slippery feel, turn red litmus blue, and also conduct electricity. These opposing properties enable acids and bases to neutralize each other in reactions producing salt and water: $\text{acid} + \text{base} \rightarrow \text{salt} + \text{water}$, with net ionic equation $\text{H}^+(aq) + \text{OH}^-(aq) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Common acids include acetic acid (vinegar), citric acid (citrus), HCl (stomach acid), and H₂SO₄ (industrial); common bases include NaOH (lye), NH₃ (ammonia cleaner), NaHCO₃ (baking soda), and Mg(OH)₂ (milk of magnesia). Safety is paramount: always wear PPE, add acid to water (never water to acid) when diluting, and flush exposures with water for 15+ minutes. Key misconceptions to avoid: confusing acid strength (ionization extent) with concentration (amount/volume); assuming all acids/bases are equally hazardous; expecting neutralization to always give pH 7 (depends on acid/base strength combinations). A systematic 6-step analysis approach ensures accurate acid-base understanding: identify substance type, determine strength, consider concentration, predict behavior, apply safety, verify with evidence. Mastery of acid-base fundamentals provides the foundation for understanding pH, titration, buffer systems, and advanced acid-base theories in subsequent sections.
